data.title: Flotek Industries: Scratched And Dented, Still A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Continuing our "Scratch and Dent" series, we return to Flotek Industries, (NYSE:FTK). The company has been fairly busy since our last article with a 1-6 reverse stock split to meet NYSE listing requirements in September 2023, an improving bill book and balance sheet, increased sales of the CnF well stimulation fluids, and some inroads on their optical gas measurement device, JP-3. Unlike many OFS companies, more things seem to be going right than wrong.

The stock rallied nearly 100% in the second quarter to $5.40 per share, but had slowly been giving some of that back in the third quarter, before another rally began in late September. I expect the decline is related to the crash in oil prices, but increasingly the company's revenue stream isn't tied solely to the rig count. Realization of that fact may be what accounts for the October rally back toward $5.00, just below which it now sits. Accordingly, we must take a look at what drove Q-2's results, and see if that may extend into Q-3, which FTK will report on November 4th.

We can't really claim a win after recommending it at a pre-split $0.77, as post-split and discounting the rallies, the stock is right where it was. Still, this is a unique player in our coverage with novel chemistry that has increasing application as gas and water cuts become more problematic, and an equipment line - JP-3 that delivers subscription-type revenue.

Could the macro market be changing in FTK's favor? Let's have a look.

Catalysts for FTK in a horrific upstream market

You don't need me to tell you that things are all "pear-shaped," in the upstream OFS market. We've discussed the bifurcation-operators are doing ok with $70 oil, but companies reliant on oilfield activity are struggling as drilling and fracking stagnate. Witness what Schlumberger, (SLB) told us the other day.

Weak oil and gas prices have caused "a cautionary approach to activity and discretionary spend by many customers," CEO Olivier Le Peuch said on SLB's earnings conference call.

And, SLB actually guided higher on International revenue-80% of its total, and margins for the year. It's made no difference as perception equals reality, SLB stock is down 50% YoY. If the King of all OFS companies is struggling, then it would seemingly be understandable that a tiny player like FTK is as well, except that it makes no sense. Like SLB, Flotek is seeing higher revenues and margins from technology sales.

You will recall our discussion on Select Water Solutions, (WTTR) and the opportunities it has in managing produced water-at the surface. Given the magnitude of the problem, you can probably imagine the interest that an oil operator would express in a proposal to minimize the amount of water cut in production. Keeping water in the ground means not having to deal with it at the surface, and that brings value for which people will pay.

At a high level, what FTK is doing in this analysis is applying destabilizing agents - CnFs and surfactants to prevent emulsions from forming as oil and aqueous fluids mix. Emulsions are oil-wet externally, so this is also a wettability problem. Prescriptive Chemistry Management means keeping the pores water-wet encourages more oil production with a lower water cut.

More oil, less water is the takeaway from the slide above. CEO Ryan Ezell discusses the YoY improvement in prescriptive chemistry sales in the Q-2 call:

Notably, we saw an 89% increase in our proprietary Complex nano-Fluids technology sales in the first half of 2024 versus the first half of 2023.

Flotek will remain at the forefront of innovation and multidisciplinary advancements as we bring new technologies to the market, including AI-driven reservoir modeling to address the impacts of water inhibition, drive preferential microfluid behavior in nanopore environments and improve the ultimate recovery of hydrocarbons from each asset.

Logic suggests that we will hear more of this come the November 5th CC.

Moving on to what the company describes as Data Analytics

Flare monitoring is the low-hanging fruit in what FTK calculates as a $220 mm TAM, annual market. Optical measurement is an improvement over traditional GC/MS type equipment that requires physical sampling to enable analysis.

There are a number of obvious markets for expansion of the JP-3 analyzer business. Two they highlight in this presentation are Custody Transfer and Field Power Generation.

The Custody Transfer application is present in every well to gathering line sale. How much, what is the BTU content, and are there liquids in the gas which need to be accounted for, are all relevant questions as oil and gas move toward storage or refining.

Most frack pumps are now Dual Fuel-meaning they run on a mix of diesel and field gas. Maximizing the field gas saves more expensive diesel, and more importantly, reduces the carbon footprint of fracking.

Ryan Ezell, CEO, comments on the growth of Data Analytics in Q-2:

Our Data Analytics segment revenue increased 22% in the first quarter of 2024. We remain focused on converting to a Data as a Service model, combined with the launch of our next-generation measurement system, unlocking significant upstream market opportunities as we expect the business to see continued growth during the third quarter.

My take on these two services is that both have a long runway for continued growth. Opportunities to help operators improve production profiles are generally easy sells-I know this from my SLB experience. The same drivers exist for meeting government imperatives with regard to regulatory adherence, and in other commercial applications they have outlined for JP-3 Verax units.

Q-2, 2024 and guidance

  • Generated total revenue of $46.2 million, a 14% increase from the first quarter of 2024, driven by a 40% sequential increase in chemistry revenues from external customers and a 22% sequential increase in Data Analytics revenue.
  • Increased gross profit margin and adjusted gross profit margin(1) to 20% and 23%, respectively, as compared to 8% and 10% respectively, during the second quarter 2023.
  • Reported net income of $2.0 million compared to a net loss of $21 thousand for the second quarter of 2023 and delivered a year-over-year increase in adjusted EBITDA(1) of $6.4 million.
  • Amended Asset Based Loan agreement increases loan commitment by 45% to $20 million and reduces the interest rate.
  • Received approval from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of the JP3 analyzer system for use in flare emission monitoring, facilitating access to a new upstream market application with an estimated annual total addressable market of $220 million.

2024 Guidance: Stronger Profitability Expectations

As a result of the Company's strong operational performance during the first half of 2024 and the outlook for the balance of the year, the Company is raising its 2024 profitability guidance. Flotek now expects adjusted EBITDA(2) to be in a range of between $14 million and $18 million, a 23% increase to the midpoint of the range when compared to the prior guidance range of between $10 million and $16 million. (Source)

Risks

The key risk in the short term for FTK is the continued weakness in oilfield activity as operators "right-size" their drilling and stimulation activity in the post-M&A craze that has resulted in a smaller market. The company has a take or pay deal (+/- $8.0 mm per quarter) with its key customer-ProFrac, (ACDC) for frac chemicals-friction reducers, clay stabilizers, and emulsion preventers, but would be better served by servicing a larger number of units. I see the primary outcome in this scenario as FTK being dead money from poor sentiment in the upstream space. This is pretty well priced in my view, so I don't see a lot of downside risk from here.

Your takeaway

It takes a bit of a leap of faith to consider an investment in FTK in the current climate. In this article, I've tried to show that they have substantial revenue generation potential that's not directly linked to the rig count.

Here's what we know. The four analysts covering the company rate it a buy, with price targets as high as $7.00. EPS estimates are for $0.07 in Q-3, and $0.09 in Q-4. They blew away the $0.05 estimate for Q-2, turning in an impressive $0.12. If they were to repeat that performance in Q-3, EBITDA would rise toward $14 mm on an annualized basis. That's about 60% above their current run rate, and would justify the $7.00 price target for the company. If we were to extend that performance to Q-4 numbers, we could see further uplift in the share price.

Accordingly, we renew our buy rating on FTK and think investors looking for speculative upside may wish to consider taking a position in the company at current levels. I think the macro themes I've outlined here are real and not tied to the rig count, and give the company an ample opportunity to increase revenues and profits.

data.title: Rising Dividend Outlook Boosting Stock Prices
data.description:
The rising outlook for dividends may be the most underrated story underscoring the rally in the S&P 500 (SPX) during the past year.

We're almost to the one-year anniversary of when the S&P 500 bottomed at 4,117.37 on 27 October 2024. From that date through the latest record-high close of 5,864.67 on Friday, 18 October 2024, the value of the S&P 500 has risen by over 42%.

We thought now would be a good time to recap how the improvement in the expected future for the index's dividends per share accompanied the rise in stock prices. In the following animation, we've presented the regular snapshots we've previously presented of the S&P 500's quarterly dividend futures from mid-October 2023 through mid-October 2024.

In particular, check out how the outlook for the fourth quarter of 2024's dividends has changed over the past year. The expectation of a dividend payout of $17.05 per share on 13 October 2024 has grown into the more current expectation of a $19.08 per share dividend payout through 18 October 2024.

How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future affects stock prices is described by this math.

More About Dividend Futures Data

For this series, we have been taking a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. So for example, as determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2024-Q4 began on Saturday, 21 September 2024, and will end on Friday, 20 December 2024.

That makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures reported by Standard and Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Meta Platforms: Time To Exercise Caution (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since I wrote my last coverage on Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) (NEOE:META:CA) stock, “Why Meta's Stock Below $500 Is A Gift,” the stock has been up 24.5% in a span of three months.

In fact, I have continuously been bullish on Meta's stock as the advertising market recovered from its slump in 2022/23. During this time, Meta's profitability improved amidst cost-cutting efforts, and Meta started materially benefiting from AI integration via improved content recommendation, driving higher user engagement and higher price per ad.

If you have followed my research since November, when I covered Meta's stock for the first time on Seeking Alpha, you would be sitting today on an 80% gain compared to the border market, which is up “only” 34%.

In my last coverage, I praised Meta's improving profit margins, even as the CAPEX spending ballooned, building state-of-art AI data centers weighing on the stock's performance, allowing investors to accumulate the shares at an attractive valuation.

However, the situation has changed, and despite Meta firing on all cylinders, the price gains are outperforming the underlying fundamentals, and investors should exercise caution, particularly as expectations are sky-high heading into Q3 earnings.

Meta's Business Keeps Delivering

Meta's app ecosystem—Facebook, WhatsApp, Messenger, and Instagram—is unmatched in the social media space. Personally, I know hardly any people who don't use at least one of these products, giving the company a dominant position with over 3.27B daily active users, worldwide.

If we look at Meta's business from a broader perspective, the company is somewhat of a one-trick pony, lacking diversification with social media advertising as its core cash cow, particularly if we gauge it against other juggernauts.

For instance, Alphabet, a parent company of Google (GOOGL), is a more diversified business that is still predominantly advertising-based, nonetheless generating revenue from Cloud, YouTube, Android, and many more avenues.

That's not to say I do not like Meta's businesses, but I enjoy Google's diversified revenue streams better. That's why I own Google in my portfolio instead, even in the face of the adverse DOJ lawsuit threatening to break up the company.

What I particularly like about Meta's business is that the company is a clear leader in AI integration and monetization, enhancing its ad-targeting capabilities, with up to 50% of Instagram's content being recommended by AI and 30% on Facebook.

These AI investments, which are partially viewed negatively by investors as they increase CAPEX spending, can strengthen Meta's core business, leading to continuous competitive advantages. Yet, I remain skeptical about the future of Reality Labs. This division could, in theory, benefit from AI integration, particularly Meta's VR headsets, but it faces stiff competition from Apple (AAPL). In my opinion, reckless spending is not value-accretive.

Meta's business has improved significantly in the last few years, with higher average revenue per user as a result of the following:

  • The company has capitalized on improving the product lineup with new features such as Reels, Stories, and the new Threads app. This has boosted user engagement and enabled the drive of higher ad revenue by integrating a more natural flow of ads into the features.
  • The higher engagement in return makes Meta's product line-up attractive to businesses of all sizes, placing their ads in front of an engaged audience. Businesses benefit from Meta's continuous investments into ad-targeting algorithms, which in return drives better advertisers' returns, and Meta's higher revenue per user.

The improved metrics are a testament to Meta's improving fundamentals, partially responsible for the 62% year-to-date stock price surge.

Even though the release of Q3 results is just around the corner, let's look at a few highlights of Q2 earnings reported in the summer.

During Q2, Meta reported $39.07B in revenue, a 22% YoY increase, outperforming analysts' expectations of $38.26B.

The EPS was also better than expected: $5.16, up 73% from the prior year and beating the forecast of $4.72.

Most of all, I am continually impressed by Meta's ability to improve the efficiency of its operations. During Q2, the Operating Margin reached as much as 38%, compared to 29% in the same quarter last year and 25% back in 2022.

In my opinion, with Meta's revenue increasing at a double-digit rate and costs and expenses remaining in check, not growing more than mid-single-digit, Meta's business could potentially achieve a 40-42% operating margin in 2025.

In the earnings release, Mark Zuckerberg was optimistic about the company's Q2 and the pace of AI deployment, stating:

"We had a strong quarter, and Meta AI is on track to be the most-used AI assistant in the world by the end of the year. We've released the first frontier-level open source AI model, we continue to see good traction with our Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses, and we're driving good growth across our apps."

The Q2 highlighted the strength of Meta's social media advertising, particularly Facebook and Instagram, which are the main cash cows of the business, with ad impressions and the average price per ad both increasing as much as 10% YoY.

Moving forward, Meta is expected to release Q3 earnings on Wednesday, October 30th after the market close, and the company previously shared a guidance of $38.5-$41B in revenue with a FX creating 2% headwind.

The total expenses for 2024 are expected to remain between $96-$99B. However, Reality Labs is projected to see increasing operating losses due to continued product development and ecosystem expansion. This is something that I do not like to see, as I already stated my concerns about its benefit to the overall company.

Management has also increased the 2024 CAPEX expectations to a range of $37-40B, which was forecasted at the beginning of the year at only $30-37B. In 2023, Meta's actual CAPEX was $30.9B.

Here, we can see Meta's commitment to continuous AI infrastructure spending. We can very likely expect further CAPEX growth in 2025, particularly as Meta aims to start deploying NVIDIA's (NVDA) Blackwell platform to support more advanced recommendations models and scaling capacity for generative AI training and inference.

Meta's management will share their 2025 CAPEX spending forecast during the Q4 earnings call. Still, I expect the spending to balloon to around $40-47B, which may weigh on investor's sentiment and potentially send the stock lower, where I would be a buyer if the stock pullbacks below $500/share again.

Even though Meta's US and European markets are already more saturated, I still see significant opportunities for the business in the Middle East, Africa, and Asia, where rising middle classes present opportunities for better-than-average ad monetization.

Analysts expect sales growth of around 9-14% over the next three years, which is absolutely feasible.

The key growth driver for the business remains its advertising business, and I expect Reality Labs to continuously weigh negatively on the company's profitability.

Naturally, higher CAPEX spending also affects profitability, but I view AI investments as value-accretive, mainly because they help the business build its competitive advantages.

If the spending falls on the lower end of the range of my expectations for 2025, Meta would have a chance to expand the operating margin even beyond the 40-42% that I already mentioned.

However, let's not forget it's not all a walk in the park. Meta's business is facing a monopoly case against its business in the US, which could potentially force the business to break up, especially if Google's remedy would follow a similar path. However, I view this as very unlikely as it would be very difficult to implement such a solution for complex businesses as Meta and Google are.

Another area of concern I have is the extent to which Meta's business benefited from Chinese retailers such as Shein and Temu's entry into the US and European markets. A slowdown in their spending may negatively impact Meta's revenue growth.

Anchor On Potential Returns

The best time to buy Meta's stock was perhaps back in 2022, during the slump, when it hit rock bottom of $88/share, trading at 9.7x its earnings.

Unfortunately, I did not pull the trigger at the time, missing 5.6x ROI.

Today, Meta's shares are no longer the bargain they once were, even compared to my previous coverage, in which I shared my bullish view.

Currently, Meta's shares are priced at a Blended P/E of 28.6x, which is a 16% premium over its 10Y P/E average of 24.7x.

Over the past decade, Meta's EPS grew at an annualized rate of 20.9%, and the growth is expected to continue, albeit at a slower rate, particularly beyond 2024:

  • 2024: Expected EPS of $21.39, 44% YoY growth.
  • 2025: Expected EPS of $24.56, 15% YoY growth.
  • 2026: Expected EPS of $28.24, 15% YoY growth.

Although Meta continues to benefit from AI integration, continuous efficiency gains boost its operating margins, and impressive user engagement drives revenue growth, the stock's forward returns are limited. The strong year-to-date rally has pushed the stock ahead of its fundamentals, prompting me to downgrade Meta to “Hold” from a previous “Buy” rating.

Naturally, for those sitting on significant gains, there is no reason to panic and sell shares of this wonderful business. However, buying the shares here presents a risk of buying at the top and the potential of a few years of underperformance.

My view is that the ROR (incl. dividends) potential is currently around 9.6% over the next two years, which is far too low to justify buying the shares at today's price. I would potentially be a buyer if the stock pulled back below $500, where it would trade at 20.6x its 12-month forward earnings.

Investor's Takeaway

Meta Platforms, Inc.'s 62% year-to-date rally has rewarded its shareholders handsomely. The company continuously improves its fundamentals by expanding operating margins, driving better user engagement through higher-quality product offerings such as Stories, Reels, and Threads, and keeping costs in check at least outside CAPEX.

Q3 earnings are around the corner, and once again, I expect Meta to deliver better-than-expected earnings. However, the stock may be pulled back by negative sentiment, particularly if CAPEX spending continues to balloon as Meta aims to strengthen its competitive advantages via AI integration.

Since my last coverage, the company's valuation has gotten ahead of its fundamentals, prompting me to revise by rating downwards to “Hold.” I advise caution as expectations are sky-high, and the returns over the coming years appear limited. However, I would be opportunistic on any weakness, pulling the stock below $500/share.

data.title: Continued Backing Up Of U.S. Rates Extend The Greenback's Gains
data.description:
Overview

The persistent rise in US rates continues to help fuel dollar gains. The euro has been sold through $1.08 and the greenback has jumped over 1% against the yen to JPY152.75. It finished last week closer to JPY149.55. So far, Japanese officials have been fairly quiet, but this seems likely to change. The US two-year premium over Germany has widened by around 65 bps since late September to return to levels that prevailed in June. The greenback is firmer against all the G10 currencies, but the sterling is straddling unchanged levels. Emerging market currencies are weaker today.

The US 10-year yield (US10Y) is up a couple of basis points to 4.23%, while European benchmark yields are mostly 1-2 bps lower. The 10-year Gilt is an exception, and its yield is up almost four basis points to almost 4.20%. More dramatic moves are at the shorter end of the curve in Europe, where two-year rates are off mostly 4-6 bps today. The two-year Gilt yield is up two basis points to nearly 4.09%. There is more speculation about a 50 bp cut from the ECB in December, but the swap market is little changed from yesterday, reflecting a little less than a 45% chance. Meanwhile, Chinese (SHCOMP), Hong Kong (HSI), South Korea (KOSPI), and Australian (AS51) equities rallied in the Asia Pacific region. We note that the weakness in the yen is not sparking demand for Japanese equities as it appeared to earlier this year. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is off for a third day, and US index futures are nursing small losses. Gold continues to rise alongside the dollar and US rates. It set a record high today near $2758.50. Lastly, after jumping around 4.5% over the past two sessions, December WTI has come back softer. It is slipping below $71 after poking above $72 a barrel yesterday.

Asia Pacific

"Feel mush, push," advised Lenin, "Feel steel retreat." And Beijing's wolf diplomacy may be taking it to heart. Australia's strategic alliance with the US while China is by far its largest trading partner is a contradiction and is the material of political novels and shows. After Australia called for an investigation of the origins of Covid, Beijing retaliated. Australia remained firm. China blinked and lifted the punitive tariffs it imposed in March. The levy on Australian wine was nearly 220%. Now that the tariffs have been lifted, exports of Australian wine to China have risen by a third in the first nine months of the year. India is the biggest buyer of oil from Russia. It is the second-largest supplier, after China, of restricted technology (microchips, circuits, and machine tools), yet China and India's relationship has been strained since the lethal border dispute in 2020. On the eve of the BRICS summit, China and India reached a deal to ease border tensions. This could set the groundwork for the first Xi and Modi formal bilateral meeting since 2022. Separately, official Chinese data show that mainland companies sold the most foreign exchange to banks since the end of 2021 last month. The depreciation of the yuan and the low interest rates have provided incentives for Chinese companies to retain their foreign currency earnings. However, the combination of the decline in US rates, the recovery of the yen, and the stimulus measures in China helped spur a short squeeze: Chinese companies to repatriate funds. Of course, media reports of Chinese banks buying foreign currencies from Chinese companies, which the US Treasury has cited in past reports, are often confused for intervention.

The continued rise in the US 10-year yield has helped send the dollar to JPY152.75, its highest level since the end of July. This is the third consecutive week the dollar is risen against the yen and the fifth week in the past six. The weaker yen is not lifting Japanese equities as it seemed to earlier this year. The benchmark three-month implied volatility has begun rising again. It is back above 11%. Official Japanese warnings may increase. The dollar is above the 200-day moving average (~JPY151.35). The next important chart area is JPY153.40-JPY155. Despite the broad strength of the dollar, the Australian dollar appeared to have forged a base near $0.6650 in recent days. However, it remains under threat today. A break could spur a quick move toward $0.6620-30, which holds the 200-day moving average and last month's low. For the past week, the US dollar has traded between roughly CNH7.1075 and CNH7.1475. It remains in that range today, and a break of that range may point the direction of the next five big figure move. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1245 (CNY7.1223 yesterday). Not counting October 8, when Chinese mainland markets reopened from the Golden Week holiday, on average, the fix has been 0.25% different than the previous day, which is on par with the pre-holiday fixings that showed an average change of a little less than 0.24%.

Europe

The news stream from Europe remains light. The euro and sterling continue to struggle to sustain even modest upticks. The double top is near $1.12 and the neckline is projected to $1.08. We initially thought that may prove to be too deep of a correction. Yet, the $1.08 area now appears to offer resistance, and the euro has been sold to $1.0780 today, and it does not look done. The market, pulling back on its aggressive pricing of Fed cuts and accepting that the sub-2% CPI gave the ECB more scope to cut rates, has spurred a dramatic adjustment of the currency-sensitive two-year interest differential. It took almost three months for the US premium to fall from 195 bps (mid-June) to 136 bps (mid-September). Yet, in the past five weeks or so, the premium has surged back to 195 bps. We anticipate that the surge is nearly over and expect a softer October employment report will help forge a top in the US premium. This may help the euro find support. The US two-year yield was a 40 bp discount to the UK in late September, as sterling reached a two-and-a-half-year high against the dollar (~$1.3435). It has approached parity and is now about a four basis point discount. Sterling has marginally surpassed the (61.8%) retracement of the leg up from the early August low (~$1.2665), which is found near $1.2965.

The next technical target may be the $1.0750 area. Options for 1.35 bln euros at $1.0755 expire tomorrow. The trendline drawn from the April and June lows is found today near $1.0765. For its part, sterling is trading a bit firmer than the euro, and there has been no follow-through selling today. Sterling recorded a low in North America morning yesterday near $1.2945. The trendline connecting the April and August lows comes in near $1.2910 today and $1.2935 at the end of the month. The (50%) retracement of the rally from the April low is slightly above $1.2865. A move above $1.30 would help stabilize the tone.

America

Given that Fed Chair Powell cited the Beige Book in his explanation, alongside CPI, in explaining why the central bank delivered a 50 bp rate cut last month, it may get a little more attention than usual. It may help cap the rising US rates to the extent it shows the disruptions from Hurricanes Helene and Milton. However, the larger event today is the Bank of Canada meeting. The swaps market is discounting about an 80% chance of a 50 bp cut today. It is tough to fade it, as the market has given the central bank an option. However, that option is not free and the prospects of a 50 bp cut (after 75 bp of cuts already this year) have weighed on the Canadian dollar. While the wage growth moderated, the September employment report was stronger than expected. Given the weakness of the Canadian dollar, and the fact that the US two-year premium over Canada is already the greatest since 1997, the latitude of Canada to run an independent monetary policy from the Fed looks limited. The Bank of Canada has surprised markets in the past, a 25 bp cut could see a jump in the Canadian dollar.

On a quarter-point cut, the US dollar could test the shelf forged last week near CAD1.3750. A break could target the CAD1.3650 area, and boost confidence that a high is in place. The swaps market is discounting a little more than a 1-in-3 chance of a 50 bp cut in December too. The forward guidance is important. A move above Monday's high (~CAD1.3850) leaves little in the way to deter a challenge on the high for the year set early August around CAD1.3945. Between last Friday and Monday, the greenback traded between about MXN19.65 and MXN20.0750. It traded well within that range yesterday. A weak retail sales report today, like the disappointing economic activity report (similar to a monthly GDP), could weigh on the peso. Tomorrow is the first half of October CPI. An uptick in the headline rate may play on fears of pass-through inflation from the weaker peso.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kanye Anderson BDC: A Solid Newcomer To Add To Your Watchlist
data.description:
Introduction

When new companies IPO, I never pay too much attention to them now. While I keep an eye on them, I hardly give it much thought to look into their financials. I've been on the receiving end of major losses investing in newly IPO'd companies.

Case in point, I lost a lot of money investing in what I thought was the next hot thing years ago, Lucid (LCID). The stock is now trading under $3 a share. And despite selling out some time ago, the losses have somewhat scarred me. But looking to freshly IPO'd BDC Kanye Anderson (NYSE:KBDC), I must say I was impressed by what I saw so far.

As a BDC enthusiast, they're pretty high on my watchlist and I may look to add them to my retirement account in the future. In this article I take a look into the company's financials and fundamentals and discuss why dividend investors may consider adding this BDC to their watchlist as well.

Brief Overview

Kanye Anderson BDC is a newly IPO'd BDC, having gone public this past May. I know other BDC enthusiasts may not prefer this, but KBDC is externally managed which means they have to pay extra fees to an external manager. And while this is something I consider when looking to invest in BDCs, it's not a showstopper for me.

Externally managed BDCs can easily waive management and incentive fees, similarly to what Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) did, permanently reducing their incentive fee from 20% to 15%. This can give a BDC a cushion on forward earnings and make them more attractive for investors as well.

KBDC's external manager KA Credit Advisors is a subsidiary of Kanye Anderson and was founded 40 years ago in 1984. They are on the smaller side with $36 billion in assets under management, compared to other large asset managers like Ares Management (ARES) & Blackstone (BX), who have $447 billion and $1.1 trillion respectively.

Early Stages But So Far, So Good

Although the company has only been public a very short period, their fundamentals have been solid so far. During their second quarter earnings this past August, their net investment income missed analysts' estimates by a penny, coming in at $0.51. Total investment income also missed by $7.33 million, coming in at $52.5 million.

Year-over-year net investment income declined from $0.56. However, their total investment income grew solidly from $40,746 million over the same period. This was due to their growth in portfolio companies, which the BDC managed to grow from 73 to 106 at the end of the quarter.

Additionally, they made a significant amount of investment commitments compared to the prior year with $171 million in new commitments, compared to just $57 million in Q2'23. Sixty-seven million dollars of this was in new companies, up more than 50% from $31 million in the prior year's quarter.

Something else that impressed me was even though the company grew their portfolio company count, they managed to decrease their leverage to just 0.53x net debt to equity, well-below the sector median's 1.16x.

For comparison purposes, this is how they stacked up against some of their externally managed peers:

Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) 1.10x

Ares Capital (ARCC) 1.06x

Blackstone Secured Lending 1.13x

KBDC managed to decrease their debt from $641 million to $622 million and had more liquidity available than total debt at $644 million. Forty-one million dollars of this was comprised of cash, strongly positioning the BDC for future growth ahead.

Furthermore, they have no debt due until 2027 with $425 million due in the months of February and June. So, from a balance sheet standpoint, Kanye Anderson BDC looks well-equipped to become a major player within the sector in the coming years.

Portfolio

Regarding their portfolio quality and borrowers, their companies were diversified, with each only accounting for 0.9%. Like several of its peers who Software and Healthcare Services make up the largest portions of their portfolios, Trading Companies & Distributors and Food Products make up Kanye Anderson's largest portions at 12.9% and nearly 10% respectively.

Their borrowers were much smaller in comparison to other larger, externally managed peers such as ARCC & BXSL. While both BDCs borrowers had weighted average EBITDAs of $165 million and $206 million respectively, KBDC's borrowers had a weighted-average EBITDA of just $58.9 million. But as a newer and smaller BDC, this is no surprise. And as they grow larger, I expect them to target larger borrowers.

This would not only likely position them to navigate economic downturns more successfully as larger borrowers are expected to see less financial distress and higher growth, it would likely drive price appreciation as investors gain more confidence in their operations.

So far, their smaller borrowers were holding up well in the higher for longer interest rate environment, with only 2 companies on non-accrual status. This rose from none in Q2'23 and this is something I will be keeping a close eye on moving forward.

Moreover, the company is likely to continue to grow by adding more borrowers, giving them higher chances of adding non-accruals. Additionally, their borrowers had a healthy 3.2x weighted-average interest coverage. One thing I did like about KBDC was their exposure to first-lien loans.

This stood at 98%, one of the highest in the sector. Below, you see how they compare to some more notable peers. Aside from Blackstone Secured Lending, Kanye Anderson BDC had the highest amongst their peers.

Company

First-Lien %

KBDC

98%

ARCC

50%

BXSL

98.6%

OCSL

84.3%

GBDC

93%

CSWC

88.7%

Dividend Coverage & NAV Growth

Kanye Anderson had ample dividend coverage of 127% during their latest quarter, despite the decline in NII from the previous year. And their management team seems confident in their ability going forward, declaring a special dividend of $0.10 this upcoming January and March 2025.

Their net asset value declined slightly from the previous quarter and year to $16.57, a discount to NAV of 3% at the time of writing. This stood at $16.63 in the previous quarter and $16.58 in Q2'23.

BDCs can often see volatility in their NAVs, especially over a shorter period of time. And considering the company suffered $0.11 dilution from their IPO, their NAV actually grew $0.05.

Moreover, for investors looking at their slight NAV decline as a negative, quality BDCs typically see solid NAV growth over time. This is a very important metric and if looking to invest in KBDC, this is something you should pay attention to as the BDC continues to grow.

Wall Street rates them a buy with a price target of $17 a share. But due to the upcoming lock-up period expiration, and lower interest rates, I think it's unlikely for Kanye Anderson BDC to see $17 in the near future.

Downside Risks

Although the BDC currently trades at a discount to its net asset value, KBDC is likely to see a further drop due to their pre-IPO shareholder lock-up expiration in Q2'25.

And because of this, along with their short track record, it's the reason I'm initiating a hold rating instead of a buy. Dividend investors are likely to receive a better entry point in the coming months. Combine this with lower interest rates, and you can see why they're a hold.

Conclusion

So far, Kanye Anderson appears to be a solid, fundamentally sound BDC. Their high exposure to first-lien loans, well-staggered debt maturities, and diversified portfolio with borrowers accounting for less than 1% allows them to likely weather volatility should the economy experience this going forward.

Additionally, their balance sheet enables them to be able to continue solid growth as they use their capital to make new investment commitments for the foreseeable future.

And despite their current discount to their net asset value price, I rate KBDC a hold due to lower interest rates and upcoming lock-up periods in the second quarter of 2025. As a result, I anticipate the BDC may see a lower share price, giving potential investors a better entry point in the months ahead.

data.title: Trick Or Treat: 3 High-Yielding REITs
data.description:
Everyone likes a high-yielding stock that has a stable business model and consistently pays dividends month-after-month or quarter-after-quarter.

Sometime these high-yielding stocks can be the delightful “treats” but occasionally, they can end up becoming nightmares.

Don't worry. Our job is to research these “high-yielders” to separate the wheat from the chaff.

Probably the most important metric to look at as it relates to dividend safety (and everything else) is to determine if underlying earnings is stable.

Can the firm continue to generate and grow its cash flow?

A conservative dividend payout ratio won’t do much good if the REIT is struggling to lease its properties and generate rent growth.

The next thing to look at is the dividend payout ratio by measuring what percentage of the company’s free cash flow, or adjusted funds from operations (“AFFO”), is being used to fund the dividend.

We always want to avoid these so-called “sucker yield” stocks.

Finally, we always look at the balance sheet to see if the company has too much debt.

One of the most significant metrics is the interest expense coverage ratio or the more comprehensive fixed charge coverage ratio, which includes preferred equity dividends and occasionally other miscellaneous expenses.

If the REIT is having a hard time servicing its debt obligations, then the dividend is probably in trouble.

Additionally, on the balance sheet, we like to see what the debt maturity schedule looks like.

What is the W.A. (weighted average) term to maturity?

Does the company need to refinance debt soon?

What is the company’s cost of debt? And so on.

A REIT with a tight payout ratio but a stable business model, including well-capitalized tenants and long lease terms, would be different from a REIT with a tight payout ratio and unstable tenants (see MPW), or a self-storage REIT that has short lease terms.

We like to look at REITs from multiple angles to access the quality of its earnings, its future growth trajectory, its balance sheet, and the safety of its dividend.

In this article, I wanted to look at a couple of high-yielding REITs that we're recommending.

Keep in mind, these are not SWAN (sleep well at night) stocks…

But they could be one day.

Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH)

Armada Hoffler is a diversified REIT that specializes in the development and management of retail, multifamily, office, and mixed-use properties which are primarily located in Mid-Atlantic and Southeast markets.

The company is a major developer who builds mixed-use ecosystems where people can shop, work and live.

While AHH owns office properties, these are typically part of a mixed-use community surrounded by multifamily and retail properties. AHH strategically builds office properties that “fit” the surrounding ecosystem, which is intended to support office demand.

AHH’s stabilized portfolio comprises retail properties, office properties, and multifamily properties which are primarily located in Virginia, Maryland, and North Carolina.

On the right side of the chart below, the company breaks down its property count, net rentable SF, occupancy, and annual rent by property type.

The company’s stabilized portfolio includes:

  • 48 retail properties that cover 4.04 million net rentable SF and have an average occupancy of 95.4%
  • 14 office properties that total 2.3 million net rentable SF and have an average occupancy of 94.3%
  • 11 multifamily properties that contain 2,492 units and have an average occupancy of 94.9%.

Many of these properties are located in clusters to form mixed-use ecosystems. For example, the company owns retail, office, and multifamily properties that are all located in the Town Center of Virginia Beach.

At the end of the second quarter, the company had a weighted average portfolio occupancy of 94.9% and a weighted average lease term (“WALT”) of 6.6 years. The contractual rent payments owned to AHH have an average term of over 6 years, which will provide some stability in the company’s earnings.

The company has a stabilized portfolio debt to EBITDAre of 6.4x and a net debt to total adjusted EBITDAre of 7.5x.

Because AHH is such a large developer, some of its capital, including debt, is tied to projects under construction that are not presently generating cash.

I’m pretty sure this is why AHH breaks down its leverage ratio for both net debt and its stabilized, cash-flowing portfolio.

AHH has a net debt + preferred to adjusted EBITDAre of 8.4x and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 1.4x.

89% of the company’s debt is fixed or hedged, and it has a W.A. interest rate of 4.6% and a W.A. term to maturity of 3.6 years.

Until COVID-19 hit, AHH had a nice streak of annual dividend increases.

The company was forced to cut its dividend from $0.84 to $0.44 per share in 2020, but has raised it each year since. The 2Q24 annualized dividend comes to $0.82, almost back to its pre-pandemic level.

The company’s dividend payout ratio varies significantly depending on whether we use normalized FFO or AFFO as the benchmark.

According to FAST Graphs, 2024 AFFO is expected at $0.83 per share, just a penny over the 2024 dividend of $0.82 per share, giving us an AFFO dividend payout ratio of ~99%. This is very tight coverage and leaves very little room for error.

However, normalized FFO (“NFFO”) paints a much better picture. The midpoint of 2024 NFFO guidance of $1.24 gives us an expected dividend payout ratio of 66.13%.

AHH uses NFFO as its non-GAAP, bottom-line earnings number and provides full-year guidance in NFFO.

Typically, under these circumstances, I would use NFFO as a proxy for free-cash flow since normalized FFO is what the company reports. However, for dividend safety it's better to look at AFFO per share as it removes additional “real” cash charges such as tenant improvements, leasing commissions, and property related capital expenditures.

With an expected 2024 dividend of $0.82 per share:

  • Using FFO of $0.25 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of ~82%
  • Using Normalized FFO of $0.34 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of roughly 60%
  • Using AFFO of $0.21 per share (annualized) gives us a dividend payout ratio of approximately 98%.

There is no doubt that the AFFO dividend payout ratio is uncomfortably high, however AFFO per share is expected to increase by 1% in 2025 and then by 6% in 2026.

If these estimates are correct, by 2026 the AFFO dividend payout ratio will be improved to ~92%, assuming the dividend remains at $0.82 per share.

Armada Hoffler is not without risk, which is why the stock trades at a P/AFFO of 13.04x, translating into an AFFO yield of 7.67%.

Additionally, the company takes development risk and is relatively small with a market cap of $897.4 million. Plus, it has a hefty amount of debt, with a total enterprise value of $2.8 billion and a long-term debt to capital ratio of 71.89%.

On the other hand, the company develops high-quality mixed-use ecosystems in major Mid-Atlantic markets and has a solid dividend track record except for the cut during the pandemic.

This is definitely more on the high-risk / high-reward end of the spectrum. However, an investor willing to ride out the next several years may benefit from locking in a 7.28% dividend yield that will hopefully be sustained and should continue to grow over the long term.

In this case, I think the recent interest rate cut, and the expected future cuts will tip the scale in AHH’s favor, especially as it relates to its ongoing developments. There is a decent amount of risk, but given the earnings and dividend yield the market is currently offering, we think the risk is commensurate with the potential reward.

We rate Armada Hoffler Properties a Buy.

Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL)

BNL is a diversified net lease REIT that specializes in the acquisition and management of single-tenant commercial properties. The company invests in various property types, but places heavy emphasis on industrial properties.

In addition to industrial properties, the company invests in restaurants, healthcare, office, and retail. Broadstone was formed in 2007 and currently has a 38.5 million SF portfolio comprising 777 commercial properties that are 99.3% leased and located across 44 states and 4 Canadian Provinces.

BNL’s business has a lot of built-in stability, with 207 tenants operating in 53 industries with a weighted average lease term (“WALT”) of 10.4 years. Plus 94.8% of the company’s tenants provide financial reporting as an extra layer of protection, and its top 10 tenants only make up 20.2% of the total portfolio annualized base rent, or ABR.

Broadstone’s portfolio generates $385.5 million in rent per year. 54.6% of the company’s ABR is generated from industrial properties, 14.0% is generated from restaurants, 12.7% is generated from healthcare, 12.6% is generated from retail, and 6.1% is generated from office properties.

BNL has an investment grade balance sheet with a BBB credit rating from S&P Global. The company has strong debt metrics including a pro forma net debt to adjusted EBITDAre of 4.9x, a long-term debt to capital ratio of 39.06%, and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 4.7x.

BNL has minimal debt maturities through 2025 and a weighted average term to maturity of 4.1 years. Plus, at the end of the second quarter, BNL had $939.0 million of total liquidity.

BNL has increased its dividend each year since 2021 and has an average dividend growth rate of 4.79% over the last several years.

More importantly, BNL can easily cover its dividend with an expected 2024 AFFO payout ratio of 81.12%, which is slightly above its average AFFO payout ratio of 78.89%.

Broadstone has plenty of attractive qualities, such as its diversified portfolio of net lease properties, its focus on industrial assets, its investment-grade balance sheet, and minimal debt maturities over the next several years.

The net lease business model should afford plenty of stability, especially in light of BNL’s WALT of 10.4 years and high occupancy rate.

BNL trades discounted compared to most of its peers in the net lease space. The stock currently trades at a P/AFFO of 12.89x, compared to:

  • Realty Income (O): AFFO multiple: 15.33x
  • Agree Realty (ADC): AFFO multiple: 18.27x
  • NNN REIT (NNN): AFFO multiple: 14.45x
  • Getty Realty (GTY): AFFO multiple: 13.92x
  • Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT): AFFO multiple: 19.62x.

Additionally, BNL pays a higher dividend yield than many of its peers in the net lease space. Currently, BNL pays a 6.31% dividend yield that is secure with an expected 2024 AFFO payout ratio of 81.12%.

Analysts expect moderate growth over the coming years, with AFFO projected to increase by 3% in both 2025 and 2026. BNL has a stable business model, a strong balance sheet, a well-covered dividend, and a discounted price.

We rate Broadstone Net Lease a Buy.

CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (CTO)

CTO has gone through a significant transformation over the last century, and more so over the last several years.

The company’s history dates back to 1902 when it was formed near Dayton Beach, Florida. The company’s former name was Consolidated-Tomoka Land Co., and it primarily sold timber and mineral rights before it began selling off its land and buying income producing properties.

In May 2020, the company changed its name to CTO Realty Growth to better reflect its core business, which is to buy and lease commercial retail properties in fast-growing, business-friendly markets, with a particular focus on sunbelt markets.

Before its name change, the company made a strategic move to spin off its single tenant net lease properties to focus more on multi-tenant retail and mixed-use properties.

The net lease, single-tenant assets were spun off and used to form Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE) which is externally managed by CTO. This gave CTO a diversified income stream made up of rent checks and management fees. (see my recent article on PINE here)

At present, CTO is a small cap REIT engaged in owning and leasing retail properties, with a particular focus on open-air shopping centers located in business friendly, fast-growing markets in the Southeast and Southwest regions of the country.

CTO has a market cap of approximately $450 million and a 4.6 million SF portfolio comprising 22 retail properties that are 93% occupied and generate $92.3 million in ABR.

For a more detailed breakdown, CTO derives 94% of its ABR from multi-tenant properties, 96% from retail and mixed-use properties, and 31% from grocery-anchored shopping centers.

Additionally, the company generates 66% of its total portfolio ABR from the high-growth states of Georgia, Texas, and Florida.

At the end of the second quarter, CTO generated 62.5% of its rent from retail properties, 33.0% from mixed-use properties, and 4.5% from office properties. The company ended 2Q-24 with an occupancy rate of 92.6% and a WALT of 4.9 years.

CTO is a bit more leveraged than I prefer, with a net debt to pro forma EBITDA of 7.5x and a fixed charge coverage ratio of 2.7x.

As a general guideline, I prefer the leverage ratio somewhere between 5x and 7x and a coverage ratio above 3x. CTO is pretty close on both measures, so I’m not too concerned about the company’s debt at this time.

Additionally, the company has no floating rate debt and a reasonable cost of debt with an average interest rate of 4.23%. One of CTO’s strongest features is its debt maturity schedule, which is well-staggered, with no immediate debt maturities and manageable debt maturities over the coming years.

Since 2017 the company has had an average AFFO growth rate of 3.14%. Keep in mind that the company presently bears very little resemblance to what it looked like in 2017.

CTO struggled during the pandemic, as AFFO fell by -58% in 2020. The following year was also rough, with AFFO per share falling by -22% in 2021.

However, the company rebounded in 2022 with AFFO per share increasing 26%. Growth has since normalized with 4% AFFO per share growth in 2023 and 3% growth expected in 2024.

CTO trades at a multiple far removed from its peers in the retail sector. Currently, the stock trades at a P/AFFO of 9.98x, which pales in comparison to its competitors.

I think part of the issue is that CTO is a small company which warrants a discount, but mainly I think the issue is that CTO is a diversified REIT, which the market tends to discount.

While CTO owns office and mixed-use properties, it derives nearly 63% of its rent from retail properties and has properties located in very attractive sunbelt markets.

Shopping center supply has been lagging since the Great Recession, and the population in the sunbelt is expected to continue its trajectory of high growth.

A large population combined with low shopping center supply bodes well for the entire sector, but particularly multi-tenant retail properties where the population is booming, as it is in Georgia, Texas, and Florida.

CTO pays a 7.81% dividend yield that is very well covered by its cash flow. The company is expected to have an AFFO dividend payout ratio of 77.6% in 2024.

Put another way, at its current price the stock offers a AFFO yield of 10.02% and a dividend yield of 7.81%, so the company is generating more than enough cash flow to support its high yield.

We rate CTO Realty Growth a Buy.

In Closing

Never forget…

The safest dividend is the one that's just been raised.

As always, thank you for reading and commenting.

Happy SWAN Investing!

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: McDonald's Shares Dip On E. Coli Outbreak
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

What investors need to know about the McDonald's (MCD) E. coli outbreak. (00:23) Spirit Airlines (SAVE) jumps on report of renewed merger talks with Frontier (ULCC). (02:17) Disney (DIS) reportedly not letting new users sign up via Apple's App Store. (03:18)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) disclosed on Tuesday that initial findings from an investigation into an E. coli outbreak indicate that a subset of illnesses may be linked to slivered onions used in the Quarter Pounder (AKA the Royale with Cheese) and sourced by a single supplier that serves three distribution centers.

The restaurant chain said that in line with its safety protocols, all local restaurants have been instructed to remove the product from their supply, and McDonald's (MCD) has paused the distribution of all slivered onions in the impacted area.

The Chicago-based company also temporarily removed the Quarter Pounder from restaurants in the impacted areas, including Colorado, Kansas, Utah and Wyoming, as well as portions of Idaho, Iowa, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, New Mexico, and Oklahoma.

The CDC said the outbreak involved 49 incidents across ten states, with one death and 10 hospitalizations being reported. The person who died after contracting E. coli was described by Colorado health officials as being "older" and with underlying conditions.

On Wall Street, TD Cowen analyst Andrew Charles said the firm's quick franchisee checks suggested McDonald's (MCD) is on top of the issue and that it seems largely contained. Charles said it is only natural to expect a short-term impact on sales.

Based on the food contamination analogies of Chipotle (CMG) in 2015 and Jack in the Box (JACK) in 1993, Charles and his team estimate that every 1% annualized change in U.S. same-store sales will lead to a $0.09 EPS impact. TD Cowen sees a worst-case scenario hit to Q4 EPS of $0.37.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) are down more than 6% in premarket action.

Spirit Airlines (NYSE:SAVE) is up 17% premarket after a report that the discount airline has had early-stage merger talks with Frontier Group (NASDAQ:ULCC).

The two low cost carriers have had recent talks about a potential merger, according to a WSJ report on Tuesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

If a deal between the carriers is agreed to, it would likely happen as part of Spirit (SAVE) restructuring its debt and other liabilities in a bankruptcy, according to the report.

The news comes after shares of Spirit (SAVE) skyrocketed on Monday after the carrier disclosed that it had landed a debt-refinancing extension with its credit card processor to extend a debt refinancing deadline to December 23.

Spirit (SAVE) attempted to merge with JetBlue (JBLU) only to have the merger shelved by a federal judge in January, who considered the marriage between two lower cost carriers as anti-competitive. Since that decision, Spirit (SAVE) shares have collapsed by 86%.

Frontier Airlines (ULCC) originally agreed to buy Spirit (SAVE) in February 2022 in a $2.9 billion cash and stock deal, though JetBlue (JBLU) ended up winning a nasty bidding war for the low-cost carrier.

Spirit Airlines (SAVE) has short interest of 33%.

Disney (NYSE:DIS) is no longer allowing new customers to sign up for Hulu or Disney+ via Apple's App Store.

Disney is now telling its would-be customers to pay for subscriptions on their own site instead of Apple's App Store. However, people who've already started paying for either service via Apple can keep doing that, the report said.

Apple takes up to 15% of the monthly fees from customers who sign up for the subscription services and Disney CEO Bob Iger back in May expressed he doesn't want to pay that anymore and wants to change the way his company looks at distribution.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

Meta wins lawsuit dismissal over child safety allegations - report

Arm to cancel chip design license deal with Qualcomm over contract dispute - report

Oil industry averts Gulf of Mexico shutdown as judge extends environmental review deadline

Catalyst watch:

  • The healthcare sector will be in the spotlight, with the start of American Society of Nephrology Kidney Week and the AACR Symposium on Molecular Targets and Cancer Therapeutics.

  • Boston Scientific (BSX) will hold its Q3 earnings call at 8am and investors will see if the company gives any updates on the Axonics (AXNX) deal.

  • Tesla (TSLA) will hold its earnings conference call at 5:30pm. A point of major emphasis will be Elon Musk's commentary on the timing of a new mass-market cheaper electric vehicle, as well as the latest updates on FSD, energy storage business, and robotaxi developments. EV stocks with the tightest trading correlation to Tesla (TSLA) after earnings include Rivian Automotive (RIVN), Faraday Future Intelligent (FFIE), and NIO (NIO). Meanwhile, Uber (UBER) has had a negative trading correlation to Tesla (TSLA) on days with robotaxi developments.

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is down 1% at $71/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.7% at $66,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is flat.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Despite increasing its dividend by 7%, Starbucks (SBUX) shares dropped over 4% due to disappointing preliminary fourth-quarter results.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Existing Home Sales

  • 11:00 am Treasury Buyback Announcement

  • 2:00 pm Fed's Beige Book

data.title: Rightmove: REA's Bid Pushes Price Too Far - Downgrade To Sell
data.description:
Introduction

I first published on Rightmove (OTCPK:RTMVF) in July 2024, just prior to the release of the group’s 1H24 result. Back then, Rightmove was trading on the London Stock Exchange (‘LSE’) at around £5.74 per share, and I rated the stock as a Hold. As at the time of writing, Rightmove stock sits at £6.32, up by ~10.1% since my Hold rating and delivering investors with a return well ahead of the ~1.3% move in the FTSE 100 price index over the same period. Although my stock calls are always made with a medium-to-long-term perspective in mind, it’s fair to say that Rightmove’s share price has outperformed my expectations in recent months.

Rightmove’s price gains appear not to have been in reaction to the 1H24 result. On the day that the result was published, the stock price fell to £5.60, down by ~1.4%. By early August, Rightmove had fallen further to ~£5.18, before staging a recovery to ~£5.50 by month end. At this point, there was little to suggest that my Hold call had been overly pessimistic. On the first trading day of September, Rightmove’s share price leapt by 27.4% in reaction to confirmation that Australian listed REA Group Ltd (‘REA’) was considering a bid for the company. In this note I’ll comment on REA’s bid for Rightmove and provide an update of my thinking about the company, including my current stock rating.

Australian Takeover Attempt

REA has many similarities to Rightmove. It runs Australia’s leading residential and commercial property websites (realestate.com.au and realcommercial.com.au). The group also has interests in mortgage broking and associated administration services, property data services, and real estate market related services in Asia. The group’s main property portal, realestate.com.au, isn’t quite as dominant in Australia as rightmove.co.uk is in the UK, but most Australians would agree that realestate.com.au is the best place to search for a home to buy or rent.

There are clearly many similarities between REA and Rightmove, however I struggle to see this fact as a strong argument as to why it made strategic sense for REA to launch a bid for the UK company. There are many similarities between the major UK and Australian banks, but I doubt even the most talented investment banker could talk the board of an Australian bank into lobbing in a bid for Lloyds or NatWest. It could be the case that REA was of the view that it was stronger than Rightmove in some key aspects (perhaps in the areas of innovation, technology and quality of management), and that it could leverage these strengths post-acquisition to deliver value for its shareholders – but Rightmove is a high-quality business, so I’m not persuaded by that type of argument. In any case, my view on the matter is irrelevant – REA’s action proves that the group was convinced that the deal made strategic and financial sense.

Commencing September 05, 2024, REA submitted a total of four offers for Rightmove, each of which were rather swiftly rejected by Rightmove’s board. The first bid, described by Rightmove’s board as an ‘unsolicited, non-binding and highly conditional proposal’ was worth ~£7.00 per share, which was actually slightly lower than the closing price of £7.08 on September 02, 2024, being the day that REA first announced that it was considering making an offer. It is interesting that Rightmove’s closing share price over the entire period of back and forth between the two companies never went above the £7.08 per share level reached before the first bid was even made – I think this indicates that the market was pricing in a low probability of a deal being consummated. Rightmove rejected REA’s fourth offer, worth £7.80 per share on September 30, 2024, at which juncture REA formally withdrew from future negotiations. Having made this declaration, in accordance with the UK’s takeover code (Rule 2.8), REA is unable to make another offer for Rightmove within the following six months (unless certain exemptions apply).

The deal appears to be, at least for now, dead. And yet, as at the time of writing, Rightmove is trading ~15% above its pre-REA-offer price of ~£5.50 per share. I can’t see any justification for this price gain in terms of changed fundamentals or improved operating conditions for Rightmove. Perhaps the market has bought in to the comments made by Rightmove’s board that were made in justifying their rejection of the final REA offer:

The Board has unanimously concluded that the Latest Proposal is unattractive and materially undervalues Rightmove. The Board has concluded that shareholder interests would be better served through the execution of Rightmove's standalone strategic plan, with the multiple paths for long-term value creation which were laid out at the Capital Markets Day in November 2023.

Source: Rightmove LSE Announcement September 30, 2024.

Has The Board Made A Bad Decision?

Although Rightmove is trading well above its pre-REA-offer level, the share price remains ~20% below the £7.80 mark of REA’s final offer. Based on my previous analysis of Rightmove, £7.80 seems like a very full price for the stock, and were it trading at that level I’d most likely have it rated as a Sell. At face value, the Rightmove board’s rejection of the REA offer appears to have been a mistake.

The final £7.80 offer from REA comprised £3.46 in cash and £4.34 attributable to the market value of 0.0417 shares in REA. Therefore, ~56% of the offer value was linked to movements in both the REA share price (denominated in Australian dollars) and the AUDGBP exchange rate. AUDGBP is currently trading at around 0.51, and at that level, I doubt that the Rightmove board would have had a strong view that the offer was subject to material downside FX risk. But what about the downside risk associated with the REA share price?

REA’s £7.80 offer was based on a then REA share price of A$200 per share. I note that in the Australian trading session that followed news that the potential deal was off, the REA share price bounced by ~5%, suggesting that the market felt that REA would have been overpaying to acquire Rightmove. REA’s share price has subsequently drifted higher and currently sits at around A$227.

Based on a quick initial analysis of REA, I would say that even at A$200 per share, the market was pricing in a lot of optimism regarding REA’s future earnings growth. I do not think that it is at all inconceivable that Rightmove’s board members were concerned that over half of the value that Rightmove shareholders were being offered by REA was in potentially materially over-valued stock. Had REA’s final offer of £7.80 been a 100% cash offer, I think that the REA board may have been willing to engage with REA to reach a deal. However, given what appears to be a very lofty valuation of REA’s stock, I am somewhat sympathetic to the decision made by Rightmove’s board to reject the offer.

Rightmove 1H24 Update Highlights

The REA takeover attempt has understandably overshadowed Rightmove’s 1H24 update. Below, I briefly summarize my main takeaways post review of the 1H24 materials.

  • Rightmove’s 1H24 result contained no major surprises, and was broadly in-line with expectations in terms of both financial and operational outcomes. It was therefore not surprising that management’s FY24E was left unchanged from the guidance provided in May 2024, being: revenue growth of 7-9%, membership growth of up to 2%, full-year ARPA growth of £75-85.
  • Key performance metrics for 1H24 were solid (see Exhibit 1), and consistent with consensus estimates. ARPA (average revenue per advertiser) of £1,497 was 6% up on 1H23 and can be expected to increase further by FY24E as agents and developers continue to upgrade packages and invest in additional products.

Exhibit 1:

  • Further progress was made in ‘Strategic Growth Areas’ (Commercial, Mortgages and Rental Services), with revenue up by 31% relative to 1H23. Rightmove’s targets for the Strategic Growth Areas have a measurement end-point of FY28, so there is a long way to go from here. Although high initial growth rates off a low base represent a promising start, it is too early to form a view as to whether or not the momentum can be maintained. Strategic Growth Areas represented 5% of group revenue in 1H24.
  • The 7% growth in revenue (on 1H23) was much lower than the 23% growth in underlying operating expenses. If I adjust for the £3.0m Coadjute write-down and £0.6m relating to acquisition costs for Coadjute and HomeViews, then the operating expense growth rate reduces to ~15.3%. Employee headcount has increased by ~12% so far in FY24, so this will explain most of the expense growth. Exhibit 2 provides a breakdown of Rightmove’s cost profile.

Exhibit 2:

  • ~60% of Rightmove’s operating costs relate to employee payroll. The right-hand bar of Exhibit 2 points to a material step up in employee costs for technology related staff in 1H24. Rightmove regard product innovation as a core strength of the group, and as shown in Exhibit 3, the company continues to deliver new products, enhancements and features to the market. The regular release of new products tends to be supportive of ARPA growth, which is one of Rightmove’s key performance metrics. In the absence of labor-saving gains from AI, ongoing innovation will require continued investment in technology staff.

Exhibit 3:

  • UK housing market conditions are rather soft – mortgage rates are still high and housing transactions numbers are soggy. Transactions are taking a long time to settle, with an average of seven months from listing to completion. At 1H24, management referred to modest signs of recovery and were hopeful that lower mortgage rates will stimulate transaction activity. Exhibit 4 shows that market conditions have improved relative to a weak 1H23, but that stock levels and new listings are not much changed relative to 2019.

Exhibit 4:

New Homes – Near-term Challenges

As shown in Exhibit 5, New Homes is Rightmove’s second-largest revenue bucket. Revenue in this channel lagged Rightmove’s other business lines in 1H24, and the near-term outlook for New Homes looks to be tough. Exhibit 6 shows that as at the end of 2023, new housing starts as a proportion of new housing completions was at its lowest point for seven years.

Exhibit 5:

Exhibit 6:

The drop off in activity for New Homes is apparent in Rightmove’s membership data. New Homes membership has fallen by ~7% since FY22, as detailed in Exhibit 7. Rightmove has been able to increase New Homes revenue despite this fall in membership numbers, thanks to ARPA growth. I will comment further on ARPA below.

Exhibit 7:

Conditions for UK housebuilders appear to have substantially worsened since Rightmove’s 1H24 update. News flow during September and October leaves me feeling rather bearish about the prospects for the UK housebuilding sector in the next couple of years:

  • In early September, Barratt Developments, the UK’s largest housebuilder flagged lower building completions in the year ahead.
  • The BBC recently reported that construction of affordable homes and social housing is being held back by a lack of funding.
  • The newly installed Labour government’s target of building 300,000 new homes per annum is looking to be extremely ambitious at this stage.
  • In early October, UK housebuilder Vistry was forced to downgrade its profit guidance after discovering that one of its divisions had materially understated building costs. Vistry’s share price has bounced a little off its immediate post downgrade lows, but remains ~24% lower than its closing price prior to the announcement.

Is ARPA Growth Sustainable?

Exhibit 8 shows Rightmove’s analysis of revenue movements between 1H23 and 1H24. Relative to 1H23, revenue increased in 1H24 by £12.6m, or 7.0%. Increases in ARPA contributed £10.4m of the total £12.6m revenue uplift. Had ARPA been flat 1H24 on 1H23, then Rightmove’s 1H24 revenue growth rate (on 1H23) would only have been 2.2%.

Exhibit 8:

Given Rightmove’s dominance in the UK real estate market, it is unlikely that the Membership numbers will grow at rapid rate from here. Indeed, as the chart above shows, Membership number movements had a negative impact on revenue for 1H24 versus 1H23. Continued growth in ARPA is therefore vital if Rightmove is going to deliver strong future revenue growth.

Rightmove’s strong market position provides it with a good deal of pricing power. Real estate and letting agents, as well as housebuilders, would think very hard before walking away from Rightmove’s platforms in response to excessive price increases. However, everything has a limit, and at some point Rightmove will hit a brick wall with its price increase strategy; economics for Rightmove’s clients can only be squeezed so much. I can see an argument for sustainable ARPA growth in the context of Rightmove delivering new product bells and whistles that have the effect of reducing costs for their clients, but such services are unlikely to be meaningful in terms of the overall revenue pie. I’m sure that management recognize that it will be difficult to achieve strong future ARPA growth over the medium term. The push into Strategic Growth Areas, which I believe comes with material execution risk, is almost certainly a reflection that ARPA headwinds could soon start to blow harder.

Closing Remarks & Conclusion

Bull cases for platform businesses such as Rightmove are often built upon the assumption that strong revenue growth can be achieved with minimal expense growth. The high rate of expense growth in 1H24 may give Rightmove bulls pause for thought. Also, if the group hopes to continue to grow revenue via increases in ARPA, continued investment in technology seems likely.

In 1H24, New Homes ARPA growth (in percentage terms) was higher than ARPA growth relating to existing homes. A slowdown in UK housebuilding is therefore likely to be a noticeable drag on Rightmove’s FY25E ARPA growth. Further, Rightmove’s New Homes division is facing a tough couple of years due to the deteriorating operating conditions for the UK housebuilding industry.

Rightmove is the dominant player in a mature market. Investment in technology has been vital to Rightmove's elevation to this position of strength. The group is a tech-heavy incumbent, enjoying exceptionally strong operating margins of ~70%. Given the starting point of Rightmove’s market dominance, I do not see how AI can realistically deliver much upside for group. On the other hand, AI tools may provide competing entities with the ability to cost-effectively develop portals and services that go head-to-head with Rightmove's offers, in effect negating the competitive advantage that Rightmove has built up over many years of investment in technology. I do not see this scenario as a near-term risk for Rightmove, but I think it is an important consideration for investors who are thinking about the longer-term outlook for the company.

Turning to valuation, I considered Rightmove to be fully valued when I first reviewed the stock in July 2024, at which point the share price was ~£5.74. With Rightmove shares now sitting at ~£6.30, valuation metrics are looking slightly overstretched to me. Combining my concern regarding valuation with the near-term and medium-term negative risk factors mentioned above, I conclude this Rightmove review with a downgrade to Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLF: Slow Down With The Euphoria
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend holding The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLF). With strong momentum, American banks are the largest in the world, and there are high expectations for their results, especially in Q4.

However, financials is the sector with the largest number of small caps in the S&P 500. The curious fact is that the highest growth expectations are in these same small caps, and many of them are not yet profitable. This raises doubts about the sustainability of the prices, especially with the current valuation.

The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF

The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is a well-known ETF among investors that seeks to replicate the Financial Select Sector Index. Below are the ETF's main holdings.

As we can see, the ETF has strong exposure to large American banks, investment holding companies and other companies in the financial sector. Finally, let's get into the investment thesis.

American Banks Are The Largest In The World

When it comes to the banking sector, the dominant countries with the most valuable assets are China and the United States. However, recent measures by China that have scared off investors have given the American financial sector an even greater shine.

Additionally, the United States has been experiencing a great moment in its stock market, and the financial sector is one of the highlights. As we can see, there are high expectations for the results of companies in the financial segment in 4Q24.

Apparently the outlook is very positive, right? However, some data left me quite skeptical about this thesis. Let's analyze each of these data below.

See The Number Of Small Caps In The Financial Segment

When we talk about the American financial sector, we usually think of large banks, characterized by their financial solidity. But let's look at the interesting data in the graph below.

It is interesting to note that the financial segment is the one with the most small caps in its composition among all sectors of the American economy. In my view, small caps are characterized by higher risks, which is not exactly in line with investors who want greater financial solidity.

What Are The Risks Of Greater Exposure To Small Caps?

We can see from the graph below that small caps logically have greater potential for profit growth compared to other companies. However, small caps are the segment with the most unprofitable companies.

I confess that the data presented made me feel a little less confident about the investment thesis in the American financial segment. However, this became even clearer when analyzing the valuation.

Valuation Is Not Attractive

A good method for evaluating a financial ETF is to perform a comparative analysis of multiples against its own history using the P/E. At its lowest point, the ETF traded at 11.4x earnings, and at its highest point 17.3x earnings, for an average of 14.3 P/E.

Currently, the ETF's P/E is 17.2x, significantly above the historical average, which makes me skeptical about the investment thesis and supports the recommendation to hold the asset. But why not make a sell recommendation? We will see this in the risks.

Potential Threats To The Thesis
